speaker: interviewer
text: Hellmuth Szprycer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szprycer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Roll 77 with Szprycer. This is an interview outside in-- it's on-site interview in-- what's the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: 28Th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, January 28 in Birkenau on the train tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hellmuth Szprycer. Child Holocaust survivor. Number 17835. 55 years later in Auschwitz-Birkenau. I'm going on the same type of train I went on-- the cattle train-- in December 17, Theresienstadt, together with my grandparents. I'm going into the past now. They must have got on this train-- about 70 to 80 people. You can imagine the first ones who jumped in here always took the best position in corners and took their place as-- as you know-- as you see here, there's this old cattle train. No comfort at all. Everybody took their position. And by the time I got on in the middle-- on the train-- on the cattle train-- I had the position in the corner there. I was all by myself, because my grandparents didn't know I was going with them. They went on the other cattle train. So you see we're sitting all down here-- or lying down. Everybody gets his position. And then suddenly the doors close, and it was all dark in here. It was like a monstrous thing. But for me, it was a moment that I thought I'm going to Birkenau to see my sister there and my grandparents there. So I had a good future look. But as we were going, the first day, we had a bit of food with us-- everybody had some food-- sugar and some-- there was some food given to us, some water in the corner there. We couldn't look out. So the first night, when we left, it was cold. It was around about 15 under zero. It was December. So and this train going towards Birkenau, which we didn't know. There was hell. Everybody was crying and shouting and pushing each other. You had to go-- when you had to do your-- your shit like on the toilet. There was a corner there. It was smelling. People are coughing. People are crying, like Yiddish people do. [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the second day, by the time we-- the second day arrived, there was already 20 deaths lying there. They put them on to another corner that they shouldn't be near us. Somebody was sitting on them, using them as chairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, so it was-- it was a nightmare. It was a nightmare-- unbelievable. Yes, it was like a nightmare. And one where people pressed on to each other, like-- you know, like monkeys when they're together-- to warm ourselves, because it was so cold. So pressing each other, we had a bit of body temperature. People spoke to me, because being a young kid, some helped me, and some-- but there was not much help to be done, because they were all-- by the time-- by the third day, they were all, you know-- they were already looking already like that-- everybody. No water then anymore. There was no-- as you can see, this wooden thing here-- the windows there. They were closed with wire on them. It was all dark. It was all dark. All dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time suddenly-- nights-- I want to say the train sometimes was stopped and put aside for an hour or two. That's why it took probably three days, because, you know, nobody had to know. I think we only travelled at night. During the day-- because people didn't have to know that there was this train going to Birkenau with these Jewish transports on it to be gassed, killed at Birkenau. So they put us aside. By the third day, when we did arrive, and you know, this door-- early in the morning-- 3:30-- the door opened. Suddenly hell. On the spots was on our-- on the platform where we arrived-- was-- must have been about 30 under zero. And the-- raus, raus! [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in that moment, everybody was shouting, water! Water! [GERMAN]. Every-- and then you saw-- then I saw suddenly everyone go. And the SS was standing on the platform with their horses, with their-- the officers. In those days, I didn't know who the officers were. And-- [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: The platform was full of ice. It was 30 under zero. Most of the people-- can you imagine all the people? How could they have gone out of here? They went in here and out. They fall out. They fall out in the-- they were already dead by the time they came onto the platform. Looking back into the-- to each-- gone-- there must have been about 20 or 30 people dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then on the platform, the SS were standing there with sticks-- baton sticks with pins in them, because otherwise you would slip. But we didn't have anything, so everybody who did arrive was flying down. It was like an ice skating rink. [GERMAN] The kapos came. It was like hell. A matter of fact, I shit in my pants-- underpants. And I saw at that moment that we were dead already. It looked like hell. That we weren't on this Earth anymore. The spot we-- and each-- I looked at other-- I looked for my grandparents. I couldn't find them. But you looked at one each other, and we had all glass eyes. Glass eyes. You can exist without food, but without water, we were dried out. And they put the sticks on us, the kapos. [GERMAN] It was really-- nobody knew what was happening. I'll tell you something. People who did go into the gas chamber at that moment, they were a very positive group. I think they were happy to go. Happy to die, because they were so worn out after that trip. They had no more [NON-ENGLISH]. And I think it was a good thing some of them-- they went in, and they didn't feel nothing, because they were-- they had no food. They had no water. So they were 3/4, probably, dead. But the ones like us who were behind, we had to suffer. We had to suffer. And that's the way we lived this-- this cattle train onto the platform Birkenau, which is really, I mean, unbelievable. It's a miracle that I'm here today after 55 years to tell you this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we wanted to look out, but we couldn't look out. There was a little opening. People did look out and say, where are they taking us? Where are they taking us? Everybody was asking the same question. Where are they taking us? So just look and say something--

words.text: we wanted to look out, but we couldn't look out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as you see that lock here-- on the inside, suddenly that door closed. And it was noisy-- metal. And then they locked it up, and everybody inside at that moment said, oy, what's happening? What are they doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was shouting, because it was such a loud noise, you know. And that metal on metal, you know? It was unbelievable-- this locking. And then we didn't even know-- I mean, we went to Birkenau not knowing, but they could have even gassed us in there. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, now, 4:30 in the morning, you can imagine, you see this big field here. We got out here. It's very cold now, but then it was 25 under zero. So you can imagine. What happened is when we got out-- we got out, this was built like a platform here. And they were standing here-- the SS was standing around here. People came out-- right, left, right, left, but everybody was running about like mad. The-- you have got to remember there were children. There were old people. And they said, left, right. Get into the row. Get into the row. [GERMAN] Nobody was listening, because people were three days without food, without water. They did-- I think they were half dead. They didn't know what they were talking about. They didn't-- they were senile. They came up to you and said, you're my son. Everybody was gone. That's why when we arrived here-- you see the immense territory here. We didn't see all that, because we were blind, having no food, having no water. So what happened is as the SS stood here, and they said to get into the rows. [GERMAN] We went-- how far we went, we didn't know, because we didn't feel nothing. We must have probably walked up to the-- just in front of the camp there where the parkings were-- were probably quarantine barracks, which were put formerly there to get us into the camp. But as I told you, getting there, people were still walking-- it was full of ice, like ice skating. People were walking there. And the barracks were maybe 10 or 20-- left and right barracks where people went in. It was cold. We got in. We were happy we were somewhere warm. But at that moment, when we went that way, and you looked-- and you looked back, there was a lot of people dead on the platform. In the wagons, they were dead. So the kapos were standing there. Dogs were there. Alsatian dogs barking, barking. There was such a lot of noise and such a lot of excitement going on that really, you know, I think if we would have walked 20 miles, we didn't know we walked the 20 miles. So we got there. At that moment, I only wanted to have one thing-- I had shit in my pants-- to see my grandparents. Because you see, the train went. And I probably got in on the last one. I must have been on the last one. It was more to the front, but I didn't see them getting out. So they were always in front of me. So when I got over to the quarantine-- it's not the quarantine. It was probably these barracks that they had to make their countings and their-- whatever it is from us to sort us out. Women in this barrack, men in this barrack. But by the time we got there, we wanted water. We wanted water. We wanted food. That's all we were interested in. I don't know how all these people-- these old people could march to foot, still alive. We didn't have much clothes on. You can imagine how cold it is. I'm standing here, marvelous dressed. I can't understand there-- 25 degrees under zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came into these barracks. And they're also full of ice when you-- you had to really go into the barracks, but you couldn't stand it outside, because you were freezing to death. So in the barracks, there were already kapos coming there and asking for any diamonds, any jewelry. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: You get [GERMAN] if you give us something. A woman was standing next to me, you know, and she said to the kapo, I have a diamond. She was standing next to me. Beautiful diamond-- I don't know-- it was three carets, four carets. In that moment, as a kid, I didn't know. 13 and a half years of age. But anyhow, she brought them glass of water. But she had two children. And these two children, she gave straightaway the water. And I begged her, give me also a bit of water. And she said, no, no. It can't be. I have my kids. I have my children. Water, I can't give to you. And that was already the moment of surviving. That started already then. The human race-- you know, at that moment, each for themselves. And so I was so thirsty. As I told you, the eyes-- glassed eyes-- everybody you saw was like from Mars. So I went outside that little barrack. And there were ice seals on it, you know. And I put myself underneath, and I-- oh, this ice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can remember. I got it into my mouth, you know, drop by drop. And that was marvelous. That was marvelous. Suddenly I renewed, like I pumped my life up again, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had my [NON-ENGLISH] full of-- I did into my pants. And I drew my pants down, and I-- my underpants-- I cut it out. And the bundle, I threw it away. And then I went to look for my mother and grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are we on the outside of the camp at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They-- actually, as I know now, and I spoke to many television people-- at that moment, the train didn't go right in. The main ramp there was not finished. The main ramp-- it was not finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of arrival-- it was in 1943. And we arrived here at the 20th of December. We left at the 17th in Theresienstadt of '43. And we arrived here December, and we arrived here the 20th, which is really right, because three days-- the 20th we arrived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this road here? The road that you see the cars on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't tell you. You see that road-- I didn't see nothing. What did I see? We got out. We were hungry. We needed water. We didn't see nothing. I don't even remember this. This, I remember, when I was in the camp afterwards. But when you arrived here, we were all death. We were half dead. I don't know how the people could have moved. I don't know people in this condition were able to move about and did it on the 25 under zero. Look how cold this is here now. You had to get out and walk. And we walked, because we were mad. Many were senile. Many people were walking-- when I met them there, they came up to me, and they asked me my name. They thought I was their cousin or I was their-- everybody. People were lying in that part there. They were lying on the floor with their hands on the ice. They couldn't get off it. And it was-- you know, it was like a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did selection take place over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selection? I didn't know from selection, but people were properly sorted out. First of all, the people who couldn't walk-- I know that many people-- old people and little children-- they moved not towards-- never towards the gas chamber, because they could keep up the movement to do it quickly so they took the old people and the little children in the van. And they took them behind the camp in the woods there. And they shot them there. They give them-- in the neck, they give-- because if they put-- if they would have put them into a crowd to go there-- into-- amongst people, it would have kept-- it would have held up the march. And the SS-- you know, the Germans were really masters in this thing. So they put these old people up and they shot them and the children, as well, and then burned them later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I told you, arriving there-- over there, it was probably 5:00 in the morning. Half of us were arrived, and by the time we got there it was 5 o'clock. So looking and looking and looking, and on the end-- suddenly, I found my grandfather. And you can imagine 25 under zero, he was standing in one of these barracks there. I remember outside, because I was left inside from these where I wanted water.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I looked on the out-- I went out of the barracks, but it was impossible. It was so cold and so freezing, and I still wanted to find my grandparents. And suddenly on the outside of the other barrack-- opposite it-- my grandfather was standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as-- suddenly in the distance, on the other side, I saw a little man standing-- 75 years of age, 76-- and I said-- without his glasses. And he was gone-- senile. He didn't-- he was moving about to the left and to the right. Because he didn't know I was on the train. As you know, I went by myself on my own accord. But when I saw him, and I said to him-- I shouted from quite a distance. I said, Opa, Opa. So then he said-- he hear it. He hear it. He was quite good for hearing properly. Hellmuth! [SOBBING] He said, Hellmuth! Where are you? Where are you? I can't-- I can't see you. Where are you? So I went near to him. And I took him into my arms. [SOBBING] And he said, [YIDDISH]. You are here. And I said, Opa, definitely I'm here. You did come. I said, yes. He said, it was so hard. But I think at that moment, that man must have felt so good, because can you imagine? If he would have stood by himself there, I don't think he could have even lasted through the shower. I said, come. Come. Come. That pressing on him, and he pressed on me. That few minutes was for me a pleasure and a satisfaction that I was there at the right place at the right moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said, sit down. I gave him the sugar. And I said I will find Oma. And I went on. And then I continue to tell you in the camp itself what later happened. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why? Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why did they do it? Why? Oh. Why? Why did they do it? Why? Why? Why? Why? I can't understand it to this day. My sisters were murdered. Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, this is the kommandantur at that moment, where the SS were sitting in. As you can see, this is the kommandantur for the SS commanders, which were here-- the officers. And I used to always walk around and move from the family lager [NON-ENGLISH]. Sometimes I had to take up letters or whatever and bring it here. Because this is my-- this was my territory here. You know, I walked about like anybody here, like the SS. So if you go nearer, you probably can see there is something set that is the kommandantur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an open space, or were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this actually is like you see it. As I've told you, every camp had in front of it barracks-- officers-- these were buildings, little buildings with entrance from one side for the officer, the obsturmfuhrer was. And he had his office. And all these different camps were standing here. And probably when they counted-- they had to count the prisoners. Every-- all the papers went here. It's like the book-- a bookkeeping office, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the main-- considered the main entrance of each lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. The main entrance. Because this is the entrance. Sometimes you see that and on the back it was-- there was no entrance. This is the entrance. A, B, C, D, E, F-- all the different camps. This is the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the selection, when people were sent into the camp, from which entrance would they have come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but sometimes, you see, the elections itself was done on the ramp, as well. The lagerstrasse, some people who arrived from some transport-- the crematoriums are right in the back there. So when they marched along here, and they marched into these camps, they were probably happy. But if they continued marching, they went into the gas chamber. But many on the ramps to the left there went directly into the gas chamber and through the lagerstrasse and through the other way. But we, the ones, when the selections were made, in these different camps, they had to walk out of here. But they never said to go into the gas chamber. They said to go into another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what always happens, you walk straight on. By the time you saw that you didn't go into a camp, because all these crematoriums burning here-- we saw them-- they knew already it's going to be finished. You know it's going to be-- the ones like us who were the whole time in this camp-- I was here a year and two months. We knew what's going to happen. So when we were on the move to go somewhere, we thought it was in the gas chamber. When we were selected, and I came out of the family lager and we walked to the left. And Mengele said yes, I'm going to make you a [NON-ENGLISH]. But was it true? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when you walked towards the D Lager and suddenly you said left, and you went into the D Lager then you were happy. You knew, oh, it's true. But when you knew you were going on, then we knew the ones who were here was the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like I told you, there was the whole family transport, who went in the beginning '44 into the gas chamber and were singing the 'Hatikvah' the whole night. And we hear them. Because you see this camp over there-- how far was it from the crematoriums?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, cold-- ice cold that night. It's even quiet now. All the sounds you can hear from-- what's going on here-- from distance, you can hear a sound. So we in the camp-- we saw everything. We saw day and night, the crematoriums burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the other kommandantur, where the Hoss and all the big shots were standing and the officers working. Go there, and you can photograph it. And every order came from here, and all the counting which was done went there, was filed in that building. Because that cross on top of it is nothing. They pu the cross on it later on. This is the haupt-- kommandantur for camp Birkenau. This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would roll call have taken place? Over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roll call-- I will show it to you later on. Roll call was in the camps itself in the barracks. That I'll show you in the barrack itself-- roll call. And the latrines I'll show you, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to take this SS thing over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, we are now in B Lager. Look around. This is where we arrived with my grandparents, which were still, at that moment, alive. And this is one of the barracks I stayed in. As you see, the chimney is still here. This is where the fire was made. Here were probably the beds, you know. In the front, you had the blockalteste who had his own room blocked with a door, which he was completely on his own. I can really see the chimney. I can see the chimney, because we used to sit on the chimney. At night when it was cold, especially in the winter, we used to sit on here. And before we went to bed, we took off our clothes, and then we were sitting. And everybody was putting up their underwear, and we were looking for fleas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because fleas-- if you had them, that meant that you could get typhus, black typhus. So we searched-- actually, I see us-- everybody here-- sitting here like monkeys, you know? Somebody helped the other, and they had to look. And then-- but we also did funny. We had sometimes bread. We organized bread. And the bread we got, we used to toast on here on the what's-it-called. And then we had some [YIDDISH]. What is that in English? You know, gar-- what we had yesterday. How do you call it? Which is very good for the blood. Garlic?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Garlic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Garlic, yes. And that was marvelous. You had that toast, and you wrapped the garlic in, you know? And then we ate it, and that was like paradise, you know? That was organized. This is what we had in those days. We had that at nighttime. And then definitely, as you see here-- I mean, it's very quiet here. But I can see the noise, which was in those days-- you know, nighttime-- a lot of people shouting. A lot of people coughing. They couldn't go on anymore. They died in their beds. The whole night you hear nothing else, but oh, [MUMBLING]. They were talking to themselves. It was a horrible nightmare. But you know, suddenly you did go to sleep, because you had to have your sleep to continue your life the next day. At 3 o'clock, the kapo, the blockalteste came out. And he-- out of his little room he had. And then suddenly he shouted, [GERMAN]. Everybody out. Everybody out. And quick. And he went to the beds, and the people who couldn't get out, he slung them out, you know. He had to be ready for the appel. By a quarter past 3:00, they brought in some food. And we got some tea-- barrels of tea. I always used to wash myself with the tea, because the tea was nice and warm. And it was 25 degrees under zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Everybody got out-- all the people moved about here. And then you had to stood in front of the barracks and was appel. And in the beginning, we all had to learn, you see, [GERMAN]. So when he said we had to go off with our hats, and when he said [GERMAN], we put them on, because it was so damn cold. So by the time we were standing there in front-- 1,700 people out of how many-- 200 or something in front of each barrack. And he counted us. And the SS  men came. Then it was [GERMAN], and we had to stay like soldiers. You can imagine with that wind now at 25 under zero. We had to keep that up until--
